ENTRY            14849   20250523                             152314849000    1 
SUBENT        14849001   20250523                             152314849001    1 
BIB                 14         74                                 14849001    2 
TITLE      High-precision Measurement of the 16O(n,n'gamma)       14849001    3 
           Cross Section using gamma-ray Detection in Liquid      14849001    4 
           Scintillators with H2O and BeO Targets                 14849001    5 
AUTHOR     (K.J.Kelly,P.A.Copp,E.A.Bennett,M.Devlin,J.M.O'Donnell,14849001    6 
           M.Paris,H.Sasaki,R.O.Nelson,J.Surbrook,C.Arnold)       14849001    7 
REFERENCE  (J,NDS,202,1,2025)                                     14849001    8 
           #doi:10.1016/j.nds.2025.04.001                         14849001    9 
REL-REF    (A,13750001,R.O.Nelson+,J,NSE,138,105,2001)            14849001   10 
           (A,23456001,M.Boromiza+,J,PR/C,101,024604,2020)        14849001   11 
INSTITUTE  (1USALAS)                                              14849001   12 
FACILITY   (LINAC,1USALAS) The Weapons Neutron Research (WNR)     14849001   13 
           facility at the Los Alamos Neutron Science Center      14849001   14 
            (LANSCE).                                             14849001   15 
INC-SPECT  The continuous (white) energy spectrum of neutrons     14849001   16 
           used for these measurements was produced via 800 MeV   14849001   17 
           proton spallation reactions on an unmoderated          14849001   18 
           tungsten target. Neutrons emitted 15 degrees to the    14849001   19 
           left of the incident proton beam direction reached     14849001   20 
           the target station of the 15L WNR flight path          14849001   21 
           21.50(1) m from the tungsten target, where the CoGNAC  14849001   22 
           detector system was stationed. Proton pulses           14849001   23 
           approximately 150 ps wide were nominally spaced at     14849001   24 
           1.779(1) micros intervals relative to neighboring      14849001   25 
           pulses. The neutron beam was collimated to 1.65 cm     14849001   26 
           diameter. The incident neutron flux energy spectrum    14849001   27 
           was measured using a 235U fission chamber.             14849001   28 
DETECTOR   (SCIN,FISCH) The Correlated Gamma-Neutron Array for    14849001   29 
           sCattering (CoGNAC). This array consists of fifty      14849001   30 
           four EJ-309 liquid scintillators in the upper          14849001   31 
           hemisphere relative to the central target position     14849001   32 
           and seventy two 6Li-enriched Cs2LiYCl6:Ce (CLYC)       14849001   33 
           scintillators in the lower hemisphere.                 14849001   34 
SAMPLE     A four-arm rotating target changer was used for both   14849001   35 
           the BeO and H2O measurements. This sample changer was  14849001   36 
           used to rotate between the target of interest and the  14849001   37 
           relevant blank for background subtraction on 10        14849001   38 
           minute intervals. Short times between target changes   14849001   39 
           are important for matching variations in beam          14849001   40 
           intensity between the targets and blanks during the    14849001   41 
           course of an experiment.                               14849001   42 
METHOD     (COINC,PSD,TOF) Neutrons and gamma rays from neutron   14849001   43 
           scattering reactions were measured using the CoGNAC    14849001   44 
           array. Coincidences between incident neutron creation  14849001   45 
           times, t0, and gamma-ray detection times, tgamma,      14849001   46 
           were formed in post-processing analysis routines and   14849001   47 
           after selection of gamma rays via pulse shape          14849001   48 
           discrimination. These cross sections are the total     14849001   49 
            (n,n'g) cross section, not the total (n,n').          14849001   50 
           Authors require the gamma emission in the output       14849001   51 
           channel, which largely removes the (n,n') to the       14849001   52 
           first excited state as discussed in the manuscript.    14849001   53 
           Then, the high-energy gamma gate cuts out this first   14849001   54 
           excited state out of the data the rest of the way      14849001   55 
           since the gamma decay mode is simultaneous 2-gamma     14849001   56 
           decay with the energy equal to the level energy,       14849001   57 
           which almost always makes gammas that are too low for  14849001   58 
           our analysis cuts. So, it's just (n,n'gamma), which    14849001   59 
           captures the strength from the 2nd-5th excited states  14849001   60 
           through the Einc range authors quote.                  14849001   61 
ERR-ANALYS (ERR-T) The total uncertainty includes the 235U(n,f)   14849001   62 
           cross section for the flux measurement, statistical    14849001   63 
           uncertainties on our fission chamber spectrum,         14849001   64 
           statistical uncertainties on the 16O gamma rays (not   14849001   65 
           negligible), and a systematic uncertainty from the     14849001   66 
           gamma detection efficiency difference between the      14849001   67 
           low and high energy sides of our gamma-rays of         14849001   68 
           interest.                                              14849001   69 
           (ERR-1) Detection efficiency.                          14849001   70 
CORRECTION The MCNP simulations show a maximum correction of ~5%  14849001   71 
           for the BeO data set at the Eninc of 9.8 MeV reported  14849001   72 
           in this work, and ~12% for the H2O data set at the     14849001   73 
           same energy. Corrections below 8 MeV were 1-2% or less.14849001   74 
STATUS     (APRVD) Approved by K.J.Kelly on June 26, 2025.        14849001   75 
HISTORY    (20250523C) BP                                         14849001   76

           physical thickness as the BeO sample by stacking a     1484900324843 
           series of thin Be disks. Empty and natural Be          1484900324844 
           background subtractions produced consistent results.   1484900324845
